<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422424 space=SYSML2P version=5 -->
## PAGE 00411: (2026x) Specification panel

- page_id: `254422424`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422424/2026x+Specification+panel
- version_number: 5
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:48:22.253+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Element management
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Specification panelallows you to review and modify properties of specific elements, such as the declared name, declared short name, feature type and value, documentation, and others. You can also review inherited features and modify or create new owned elements in the appropriate compartments, which are equivalent to those displayed in view symbols. To review all element properties, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification window' space=''] page.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Specification panel displays the *vehicleMassLimitationRequirement*element's specification information.

#### Working with the Specification panel

##### Opening the Specification panel

To open the Specification panel, do one of the following

- In the Textual Editor, right-click an element and in the shortcut menu, click Specification .
- Double-click an element in the Containment tree or its symbol in the view Pane.
- Select an element in the Containment tree or its symbol on the View Pane and press Enter.
- Right-click an element in the Containment tree or its symbol on the View Pane and click **Specification**in its shortcut menu.
- Select an element in the Containment tree or its symbol on the View Pane, then in the main menu, click **Window**> **Specification**.
- To open the Specification panel for a specific element displayed in the compartments section when the Specification panel is already open for another element, hover over the needed element and click the **Navigate [IMAGE alt='' src='']**button. **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

##### Switching between Specification panel tabs

Opening the Specification panel opens the Properties tab by default.

To switch between Specification panel tabs

- Click the needed tab in the tab bar. [ATTACHMENT filename='tabs_bar.png']

#### Properties tab

The Properties tab allows you to modify the element properties and manage related element compartments.

##### Working with element properties via the Properties tab of the Specification panel

###### Modifying the declared name of the element via the Specification panel

To modify the declared name of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, p lace the cursor on the Declared Name input field.
3. Edit the declared name as needed.
4. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the declared short name of the element via the Specification panel

To modify the declared short name of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, p lace the cursor on the Declared Short Name input field.
3. Edit the declared short name as needed.
4. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the feature type via the Specification panel

To modify the feature type via the Specification panel

1. .
2. Do one of the following:
  1. Drag and drop the typing element from the Containment tree on the Type field in the Specification panel.
  2. Select the typing element symbol in a view and use its Drag and Drop handler to drag and drop it on the Type field in the Specification panel. If the type is already set, dragging and dropping the new type replaces the set type with the new one.To add additional types, hold the CTRL/Command key while dragging and dropping the additional type.To delete the set type, click the **Delete** [IMAGE alt='' src='']****button next to the **Type**input field.
3. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying additional properties via the Specification panel

Depending on the element, additional properties may be displayed in the Properties section of the Specification panel. These properties can be specified in the same way as for the .

- Result Expression - displays a r ead-only string of the constraint expression.
- Asserted Constraint - displays asserted constraint's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Constraints' space=''] page.
- Assumed Constraint - displays assumed constraint's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Constraints' space=''] page.
- Required Constraint - displays required constraint's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Constraints' space=''] page.
- Exhibited State - displays exhibited state's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Exhibit state' space='']****page.
- Use Case Included - displays included use cases's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Include use case' space=''] page.
- Performed Action - displays performed action's element name. See the****[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Perform action' space=''] page.

###### Modifying the feature value via the Specification panel

To modify the feature value via the Specification panel

1. . In the Value subsection:
  1. To modify the value modifier, click the first dropdown button and click the needed modifier. [ATTACHMENT filename='Modifying_the_feature_value_of_the_element_via_the_Specification_panel.png']
  2. Click the up or down arrow buttons in the second field to modify the feature value. The modification of both fields is available only when the value is already specified. Otherwise, it is read-only.To delete the specified value, click the **Delete** [IMAGE alt='' src='']****button next to the field.
2. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the documentation of the element via the Specification panel

To modify the documentation of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, p lace the cursor on the Documentation input field.
3. Edit the declared name as needed. To delete the specified documentation, click the **Delete** [IMAGE alt='' src='']****button next to the **Documentation**input field. Deleting the field content (using Backspace or Delete keys) does not delete the documentation but rather specifies it as empty.
4. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Modifying the multiplicity of the element via the Specification panel

To modify the multiplicity of the element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section, click the input field under the Multiplicity subsection and do one of the following:
  1. Select the needed multiplicity in the dropdown menu.
  2. Enter the needed multiplicity number.
3. Click anywhere outside the edited input field for changes to take effect.

###### Creating an element via the Specification panel

To create an element via the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears.
3. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu. The selected element is created in the appropriate compartment. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Working with elements via compartments in the Properties tab of the Specification panel

###### Creating an element via the compartment in the Specification panel

To create an element via the compartment in the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the appropriate compartment section's bar, click the **Create <element name>** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button (e.g., *Create attribute*). The element is created in the appropriate compartment. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Deleting an element via the compartment in the Specification panel

To delete an element via the compartment in the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the appropriate compartment's section, hover over the element you want to delete.
3. Click the **Delete [IMAGE alt='' src='']**button. The element is deleted from the appropriate compartment. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Redefining inherited elements via the compartment in the Specification panel

To redefine inherited elementsvia the compartment in the Specification panel

1. .
2. In the appropriate compartment's section, hover over the inherited element you want to redefine.
3. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='expand_button.png'] button and in its submenu, click the Redefine command. [ATTACHMENT filename='Redefining_inherited_elements_via_the_compartment_in_the_Specification_panel.png']

###### Redefining inherited features via the compartment in the Specification panel

The element features displayed in the compartments are grouped under labels indicating inherited and redefined features.

To redefine inherited features via the compartment in the Specification panel

1. .
2. To redefine the inherited feature with a set default value, h over over the inherited feature you want to redefine and click the **Navigate**button **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**. The Specification panel is opened for the selected feature.
3. Click the **Redefine** button in the informational message at the top of the Specification panel. The properties in the panel are read-only until redefinition is done to prevent modification of non-redefined property values. Redefining the inherited metadata feature without a set default valueTo redefine the inherited metadata feature without a set default value, click the **Add** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button in the metadata compartment section's bar. In the submenu, hover over the needed metadata, and in its submenu, click the feature you want to redefine. The feature is redefined and displayed in the metadata compartment under the label of the applied metadata's name. NOTE:The **Add**button is not displayed if all features are already redefined or the element inherits a feature with a set default value. Once done, you can modify the redefined feature's values via the procedure.

#### Navigation tab

The Navigation tab allows you to easily navigate from a selected element to its related elements based on their relationships. The tab organizes related elements into categories, such as Subsetted Feature, Redefined Feature, Flow, Connection, Type, etc.

##### Navigating elements via the Navigation tab

To navigate elements via the Navigation tab

1. Click the Navigation tab in the tabs bar.
2. Hover over the needed element and do one of the following:
  1. Click the **Navigate**button **[IMAGE alt='' src='']**to open the element's Specification panel.
  2. Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='expand_button.png'] button and in its submenu, click the Select in Containment Tree command.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The shortcut menu appears.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu. The selected element is created in the appropriate compartment.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Creating_an_element_via_the_Specification_pane.png" /></ac:image></span></li></ol><h3>Working with elements via compartments in the Properties tab of the Specification panel</h3><h4>Creating an element via the compartment in the Specification panel</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To create an element via the compartment in the Specification panel</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the appropriate compartment section's bar, click the <strong>Create &lt;element name&gt;</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_compartment_via_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> button (e.g., <em>Create attribute</em>). The element is created in the appropriate compartment.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Creating_an_element_via_the_compartment_in_the_Specification_pane.png" /></ac:image></span></li></ol><h4>Deleting an element via the compartment in the Specification panel</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To delete an element via the compartment in the Specification panel</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the appropriate compartment's section, hover over the element you want to delete.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Click the <strong>Delete <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="delete_element_in_compartment_via_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button.</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> The element is deleted from the appropriate compartment.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Deleting_an_element_via_the_compartment_in_the_Specification_pane.png" /></ac:image></span></li></ol><h4>Redefining inherited elements via the compartment in the Specification panel</h4><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To redefine inherited elements </span>via the compartment in the Specification panel</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the appropriate compartment's section, hover over the inherited element you want to redefine.</span></li><li>Click the <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="expand_button.png" /></ac:image> button and in its submenu, click the <strong>Redefine </strong>command. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Redefining_inherited_elements_via_the_compartment_in_the_Specification_panel.png" /></ac:image></li></ol><h4>Redefining inherited features via the compartment in the Specification panel</h4><p>The element features displayed in the compartments are grouped under labels indicating inherited and redefined features.</p><p>To redefine inherited features via the compartment in the Specification panel</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d6be1781-801c-4a88-8260-d84230dc1e5c">To redefine the inherited feature with a set default value, h</ac:inline-comment-marker></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">over over the inherited feature you want to redefine and click the <span><strong>Navigate </strong>button <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="navigate_in_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image></strong>. The Specification panel is opened for the selected feature. </span></span></li><li data-uuid="0d90a117-c762-402e-b9da-c30ec531be31"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span>Click the <strong>Redefine</strong> button in the informational message at the top of the Specification panel. <br /></span></span><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3c63ca14-7850-4f57-9b7c-18fa5d65de4b"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>The properties in the panel are read-only until redefinition is done to prevent modification of non-redefined property values.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="706cb0f6-ee2c-4d53-8487-3beba4604566"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Redefining the inherited metadata feature without a set default value</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To redefine the inherited metadata feature without a set default value, c</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">lick the <strong>Add</strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_element_in_compartment_via_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> button in the metadata compartment section's bar. In the submenu, hover over the needed metadata, and in its submenu, click the feature you want to redefine. The feature is redefined and displayed in the metadata compartment under the label of the applied metadata's name. NOTE: </span>The <strong>Add </strong>button is not displayed if all features are already redefined or the element inherits a feature with a set default value.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Once done, you can modify the redefined feature's values via the <ac:link ac:anchor="Modifying the feature value via the Specification panel" /> procedure.</span></li></ol><h2>Navigation tab</h2><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">The Navigation tab allows you to easily navigate from a selected element to its related elements based on their relationships. The tab organizes related elements into categories, such as Subsetted Feature, Redefined Feature, Flow, Connection, Type, etc.</span></p><h3><span>Navigating elements via the Navigation tab</span></h3><p><span>To navigate elements via the Navigation tab</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span>Click the Navigation tab in the tabs bar.</span></li><li><span>Hover over the needed element and do one of the following:</span><ol><li><span>Click the <strong>Navigate </strong>button <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="navigate_in_specification_pane_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong></span>to open the element's Specification panel.</span></li><li><span>Click the </span><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="expand_button.png" /></ac:image> button and in its submenu, click the <strong>Select in Containment Tree</strong> command.</li></ol></li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422918 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00412: (2026x) Specification window

- page_id: `254422918`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422918/2026x+Specification+window
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-17T15:09:01.825+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Element management > (2026x) Specification panel
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Specification window allows you to review all properties of the specific element.

The Specification window is comprised of two columns:

- The Property column displays the name of the property.
- The Value column displays the corresponding value of the property.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Example of a Specification window for a *drone in any mission* part.

##### Opening the Specification window

To open the Specification window

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''].
2. In the Navigation bar of the Specification panel, click the **All Properties [IMAGE alt='' src='']** button. The Specification window opens.

##### Sorting element properties alphabetically

To sort element properties alphabetically

1. In the Specification window, click the Property column header. The Sorting icon is displayed in the header indicating the current sorting order. Sorting order types[IMAGE alt='' src=''] indicates that properties are sorted alphabetically in ascending order. This is the default mode.[IMAGE alt='' src=''] indicates that properties are sorted alphabetically in descending order.
2. Click the Property column header to change the sorting order.

##### Filtering element properties and values

The Specification window filter allows you to filter element properties and values, allowing you to locate and display specific properties and their values in the Specification window.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Filtering element properties

To filter element properties

1. In the Specification window, right-click the Property column header and click Filter Column in the shortcut menu.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. Expand the Filter by Values section. Do one of the following:
    1. In the Values column, select the check boxes for the properties you want to display in the Specification window.
    2. Type the property name in the input field to narrow the search, select the Apply filter on refined values check box, and select the check boxes for the properties you want to display in the Specification window.
  2. Expand the Filter by Conditions section.
    1. Select the condition from the dropdown menu and type the keyword for the property you are looking for in the input field.
3. Click OK .

###### Filtering element property values

To filter element property values

1. In the Specification window, right-click the Value column header and click Filter Column in the shortcut menu.
2. Do one of the following:
  1. Expand the Filter by Values section. Do one of the following:
    1. In the Values column, select the check boxes for the property values you want to display in the Specification window.
    2. Type the property value in the input field to narrow the search, select the Apply filter on refined values check box, and select the check boxes for the property values you want to display in the Specification window.
  2. Expand the Filter by Conditions section.
    1. Select the condition from the dropdown menu and type the keyword for the property value you are looking for in the input field.
3. Click OK .

###### Resetting the filter

To reset the filter

1. Right-click the column header with the applied filter.
2. In the shortcut menu, do one of the following:
  1. Click Reset Column Filter .
  2. Click Filter Column , then Reset . Click OK .

##### Modifying element properties via the Specification window

You can modify string, number, and boolean values for properties in the Specification window

To modify element properties via the Specification window

1. .
2. Locate the property you want to modify.
3. In the Value column, do one of the following:
  1. To modify a string or number value, click the cell and type the needed value.
  2. To modify a boolean value, select the check box to set the value to true or deselect it to set the value to false . Read-only properties cannot be modified.
4. Click Close .

##### Toggling library elements' visibility in the Specification Window

To toggle library elements' visibility in the Specification Window

1. .
2. Toggle the Display library elements button at the top of the window.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The Specification window allows you to review all properties of the specific element.</span></p><p style="text-align: left;">The Specification window is comprised of two columns:</p><ul><li style="text-align: left;">The <strong>Property </strong>column displays the name of the property.</li><li style="text-align: left;">The <strong>Value</strong> column displays the corresponding value of the property.</li></ul><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="specification_window.png" /></ac:image></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Example of a Specification window for a <em>drone in any mission</em> part. </span></h6><h3>Opening the Specification window</h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To open th<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="16b2fc84-bb66-46b2-9196-f1f0bf77b0f2">e Specification window</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><hr /><ol><li style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification panel"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Specification panel" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification panel]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></li><li style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the Navigation bar of the Specification panel, click the <strong>All Properties <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="All_properties_button.png" /></ac:image></strong> button. The Sorting icon is displayed in the header indicating the current sorting order.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f1484ef-48ac-48c0-a06a-a94104db72ba"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Sorting order types</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="ascending_order.png" /></ac:image> indicates that properties are sorted alphabetically in ascending order. Do one of the following:<ol><li>In the <strong>Values</strong> column, select the check boxes for the properties you want to display in the Specification window.</li><li>Type the property name in the input field to narrow the search, select the <strong>Apply filter on refined values</strong> check box, and select the check boxes for the properties you want to display in the Specification window.</li></ol></li><li>Expand the <strong>Filter by Conditions</strong> section.<ol><li>Select the condition from the dropdown menu and type the keyword for the property you are looking for in the input field. </li></ol></li></ol></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><h4>Filtering element property values</h4><p>To filter element property values</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Specification window, right-click the <strong>Value </strong>column header and click <strong>Filter Column</strong> in the shortcut menu.</li><li>Do one of the following:<ol><li>Expand the <strong>Filter by Values</strong> section. Do one of the following:<ol><li>In the <strong>Values</strong> column, select the check boxes for the property values you want to display in the Specification window.</li><li>Type the property value in the input field to narrow the search, select the <strong>Apply filter on refined values</strong> check box, and select the check boxes for the property values you want to display in the Specification window.</li></ol></li><li>Expand the <strong>Filter by Conditions</strong> section.<ol><li>Select the condition from the dropdown menu and type the keyword for the property value you are looking for in the input field.</li></ol></li></ol></li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><h4>Resetting the filter</h4><p>To reset the filter</p><hr /><ol><li>Right-click the column header with the applied filter.</li><li>In the shortcut menu, do one of the following:<ol><li>Click <strong>Reset Column Filter</strong>.</li><li>Click <strong>Filter Column</strong>, then <strong>Reset</strong>. Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol></li></ol><h3>Modifying element properties via the Specification window</h3><p>You can modify string, number, and boolean values for properties in the Specification window</p><p>To modify element properties via the Specification window</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification window"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>Locate the property you want to modify.</li><li>In the <strong>Value </strong>column, do one of the following:<ol><li>To modify a string or number value, click the cell and type the needed value.</li><li>To modify a boolean value, select the check box to set the value to <em>true </em>or deselect it to set the value to <em>false</em>.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ded34fb1-83b3-4e08-9553-7adb9ba29654"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Read-only properties cannot be modified.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong>.</li></ol><h3>Toggling library elements' visibility in the Specification Window</h3><p>To toggle library elements' visibility in the Specification Window</p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="bd543745-5a5c-48b4-a2b3-3fc51cca03fb"><ac:link ac:anchor="Opening the Specification window"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Open the Specification window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li data-uuid="a02e0459-8834-477a-ae40-c86e2a04782a">Toggle the <strong>Display library elements</strong> button at the top of the window.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=263094409 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00413: (2026x) Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective

- page_id: `263094409`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/263094409/2026x+Specifying+requirement+verification+usages+for+verification+case+objective
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-11T20:19:35.274+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Analysis & Verification > (2026x) Verification case
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In addition to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Requirement constraints' space='']usages allowed in any requirement usage, the objective of a verification case may also have**requirement verification usages**, which indicate the requirements to be verified by the verification case. Since the requirement verification usage is a subrequirement of the objective, it is automatically considered a required constraint of its containing objective.

There are two ways a requirement verification usage can be specified for a verification case objective:

50%

- by referencing the requirement usage defined elsewhere in the model.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the verify keyword (unnamed element) or the verify requirement keyword (named element) . [ATTACHMENT filename='Specifying requirement usages defined elsewhere in the model as requirement verification usages for verification case objective.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 

50%

- by containing an owned requirement usage, i.e., the verified requirement is the owned requirement usage itself.
  - The element symbol is displayed with the verify requirement keyword. [ATTACHMENT filename='Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective via the owned verify requirement usage itself as the verified requirement.png']
  - See the procedures:
    - :
      - 

##### Specifying requirement usages defined elsewhere in the model as requirement verification usages for verification case objective

###### Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective via the Textual Editor

To specify requirement verification usages for verification case objective via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor within the curly braces of the verification case objective's body, do one of the following :
  1. For an unnamed requirement verification usage, declare the verify keyword followed by the name of the requirement you want to reference.
  2. For a named requirement verification usage, declare the verify requirement keyword, name the new usage, and then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Reference subsetting' space=''] the requirement you want to reference.
2. (Optional) Specify the requirement in the element's body.
3. Click the Synchronize button. requirementdefVehicleMassRequirement{ 
//... 
}requirementvehicleMassRequirement{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
inmassActual:>ISQ::mass=vehicle.mass; 
doc/* The vehicle mass shall be less than or equal to 2500 kg. */ 
requireconstraint{ 
massActual<=2500[SI::kg] 
} 
}// step 1.a.verificationdefVehicleMassTest{ 
subjecttestVehicle:Vehicle; 
objectivevehicleMassVerificationObjective{ 
verifyvehicleMassRequirement;// unnamed requirement verification usage reference subsets another requirement usage 
} 
returnverdict:VerificationCases::VerdictKind; 
}// step 1.bverificationdefVehicleMassTest{ 
subjecttestVehicle:Vehicle; 
objectivevehicleMassVerificationObjective{ 
verifyrequirementvehicleMassRequirementForTest::>vehicleMassRequirement;// named requirement verification usage reference subsets another requirement usage 
} 
returnverdict:VerificationCases::VerdictKind; 
}

##### Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective via the owned verify requirement usage itself as the verified requirement

###### Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective via the Textual Editor2Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective via the Textual Editor

To specify requirement verification usages for verification case objective via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor within the curly braces of the verification case objective's body, declare the verify requirement keyword, specify the element name, then the requirement body.
2. Click the Synchronize button. verificationdefVehicleMassTest{ 
subjecttestVehicle:Vehicle; 
objectivevehicleMassVerificationObjective{ 
verifyrequirementvehicleMassRequirement{// new verification case objective-owned requirement verification usage 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
inmassActual:>ISQ::mass=vehicle.mass; 
doc/* The vehicle mass shall be less than or equal to 2500 kg. */ 
requireconstraint{ 
massActual<=2500[SI::kg] 
} 
} 
} 
returnverdict:VerificationCases::VerdictKind; 
}

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Since the requirement verification usage is a subrequirement of the objective, it is automatically considered a required constraint of its containing objective.</span></p><p>There are two ways a ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Specifying requirement usages defined elsewhere in the model as requirement verification usages for verification case objective</h3><h4>Specifying requirement verification usages for verification case objective via the Textual Editor</h4><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">To specify requirement verification usages for verification case objective via of the verification case objective's body, do one of the following</span>:<ol><li data-uuid="dfe3c80d-707b-43c1-bd45-3b526645b3a8">For an unnamed <span>requirement verification usage, </span>declare the <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">verify</em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> keyword followed by the name of the requirement you want to reference.</span></li><li data-uuid="33828838-453f-473a-bb48-9ab77e39f948"><span>For a named requirement verification usage, </span>declare the <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">verify requirement </em><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">keyword, name the new usage, and then <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Reference subsetting" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[reference subset]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the requirement you want to reference.</span></li></ol></li><li data-uuid="24da7a7e-375d-4bb1-8176-c33f04fc4fbf">(Optional) Specify the requirement in the element's body.</li><li data-uuid="0bc8ddfa-d084-4d23-bd7e-5575eb29ace8">Click the <strong>Synchronize<span> </span></strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422894 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00414: (2026x) Start and done actions

- page_id: `254422894`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422894/2026x+Start+and+done+actions
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:21:20.674+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Actions > (2026x) Controlling actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A start action and a done action represent the start and end of an action sequence.

##### Creating start and done actions

###### Creating start and done actions via the Textual Editor

To create start and done actions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:
  1. *start*for a start action.
  2. done for a done action. Although *start*and *done*appear as element names in the Textual Editor, they are keywords for start and done actions. However, they cannot be named or renamed.
2. Click the Synchronize button. actiontransportPassenger{ 
firststart; 
...thendone;}

###### Creating control nodes via the view palette

To create control nodes via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Actions group, click one of the following:
  1. **start** or a start action.
  2. done for a done action.
2. Click in the view pane where you want to create the action symbol.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A start action and a done action represent the start and end of an action sequence.</p><h3>Creating start and done actions</h3><h4>Creating start and done actions via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To create start and done actions via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, place the cursor where you want to create the element and declare the keyword:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><em>start </em>for a start action.</p></li><li><em>done </em>for a done action.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3e54883e-0663-4bf2-83f9-6513529d0d6c"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Although <em>start </em>and <em>done </em>appear as element names in the Textual Editor, they are keywords for start and done actions. However, they cannot be named or renamed.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol></li><li>Click the <strong>Synchronize </strong>button.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae630f5a-39fc-43ac-aa7b-b45e25fd9b91"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">action</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">transportPassenger</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> {</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">first</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">start</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;</span><br /><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-green-bolder,#164b35);">...<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">    </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue,#0055cc);">then</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"> </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text-accent-blue-bolder,#09326c);">done</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">;<br /></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);">}</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><h4>Creating control nodes via the view palette</h4><p>To create <span>control nodes via the view palette</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the view palette, under the Actions group, click one of the following:<ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">start</strong> or a start action.</p></li><li><strong>done </strong>for a done action.</li></ol></li><li>Click in the view pane where you want to create the action symbol.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422585 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00415: (2026x) State decomposition

- page_id: `254422585`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422585/2026x+State+decomposition
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:23:24.009+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) States
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

State definitions and state usages can be decomposed into lower-level states to create a hierarchy of states. You can define a state usage by a state definition, and a state usage can be subsetted or redefined. This provides enhanced flexibility to modify a hierarchy of state usages to adapt to its context.

##### Decomposing state definitions

You can represent the next level of decomposition of a state definition using the feature typing (*defined by*) relationship.

###### Decomposing state definitions via the Textual Editor

To decompose state definitions via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the state usage that you want to define, nested within a state definition.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Feature typing' space=''] .

statedefonOffStates{ 
stateon:OnState; // state usage, owned by a state definition, defined by another state definition 
stateoff:OffState; 
} 
statedefOnState; 
statedefOffState;

###### Displaying decompositions of state definitions in a view

To display decompositions of state definitions in a view

- In a view, select the state definition symbol and the state usage symbol it defines, then use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command under the In-Between Relationships category . The feature typing relationship is displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Decomposing state usages

You can display a state tree representing a composition of a state from other states.

###### Decomposing state usages via the Textual Editor

To decompose state usages via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the body of the state you want to decompose, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating states' space=''] .
2. Click the Synchronize button.

statedeviceStates:onOffStates{ // the owning state usage with nested state usages 
stateon:OnState; 
stateoff:OffState; 
}

###### Decomposing state usages via the view palette by creating a composite state

To decompose state usages via the view palette by creating a composite state

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating states' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the state for which you want to create a composite state.
2. Create a composite state in one of the following ways:
  1. Via the view palette (state symbol option):
    1. In the view palette, under the States group, click the state button to create a state usage element.
    2. Click on the state symbol displayed in the view pane to create a state owned by another.
  2. Via the view palette (feature membership option):
    1. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
    2. First, click on the state symbol displayed in the view pane and then click anywhere in the view pane to create a state owned by another.

###### Decomposing state usages via the view palette by designating an existing state as a composite one

To decompose state usages via the view palette by designating an existing state as a composite one

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating states' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the states you want to designate as the owner and the owned.
2. In the view palette, under the Other Relationships group, click the feature membership button.
3. First, click on the state symbol you want to designate as the owner, then the one you want to designate as the owned one.

###### Displaying decompositions of state usages in a view

To display decompositions of state usages in a view

- In a view, select the owning state usage symbol and use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command under the Tree-View Style category. The feature membership and the owned features (state usages) are displayed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Reusing and modifying the state tree

A state can subset another state, facilitating state tree reuse and modification. The subsetting state inherits the state decomposition and other features. You can then modify the inherited states and features through redefinition and subsetting in addition to adding new states.

###### Reusing and modifying the states tree via the Textual Editor

To reuse and modify the states tree via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating states' space=''] you want to reuse, then [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Subsetting' space=''] it.
2. (Optional) [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Redefinition' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Subsetting' space=''] the state features in the element body.
3. Click the Synchronize button.

statedeviceStates:onOffStates{// subsetted state 
stateon:OnState; 
stateoff:OffState; 
}

state heaterStates :> deviceStates {// a state reuses the states tree by subsetting another state 
 state :>> on; 
 state :>> off; 
}

###### Reusing the states tree via the view palette

To reuse the states tree via the view palette

1. In a view, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating states' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] the state whose decomposition you want to reuse.
2. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, click the subsetting button.
3. Click on the state you want to denote as the subsetting one and then click on the state you want to denote as the subsetted (reused) one.

###### Displaying the state tree reuse in a view

To display the state tree reuse in a view

1. In a view, select the subsetting and the subsetted state usage symbols, then u se the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command under the In-Between Relationships category . The subsetting relationship is displayed.
2. (Optional) To display owned features of the subsetting or subsetted states, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements in symbolic views' space=''] command. The owned features (state usages) are displayed. [ATTACHMENT filename='Displaying the state tree reuse on a diagram.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>State definitions and state usages can be decomposed into lower-level states to create a hierarchy of states. You can define a state usage by a state definition, and a state usage can be subsetted or redefined. This provides enhanced flexibility to modify a hierarchy of state usages to adapt to its context.</p><h3>Decomposing state definitions</h3><p>You can represent the next level of decomposition of a state definition using the feature typing (<em>defined by</em>) relationship.</p><h4>Decomposing state definitions via the Textual Editor</h4><p>To decompose state definitions via the Textual Editor</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Textual Editor, identify the state usage that you want to define, nested within a state definition.</li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="Defining an element via the Textual Editor"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Feature typing" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Define the state by the state definition]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. </li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="01f0aaba-0b98-4288-8292-1bf9e09c9058"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254421989 space=SYSML2P version=1 -->
## PAGE 00417: (2026x) Status Line

- page_id: `254421989`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254421989/2026x+Status+Line
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-09-12T14:33:04.013+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Project management > (2026x) Modeling tool environment > (2026x) User interface
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The Status line is located at the bottom of the modeling tool window. It displays various indications of active notifications, errors, the progress of currently running tasks, and some useful commands.

| Status bar icon | Status bar icon name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Zoom Out | Click to zoom out on the current view. |
|  | Zoom In | Click to zoom in on the current view. |
|  | View Zoom Slider | Click the zoom slider's cursor to zoom in or out of the current view. |
|  | Fit To Window | Click to fit the current view in the window. |
|  | Zoom 1:1 | Click to restore the view to its original size. |
|  | Enter Presentation Mode | Click to enter the presentation mode. |
|  | Open Notification Window | Click to open the notification window. |
|  | Background Tasks | Click to view the background tasks. |
|  | Memory Monitor | Displays the memory used by the modeling tool. |

##### Managing the display of the Status line's commands

To manage the display of some of the Status line's commands

- In the main menu, click View > Status line .
- In the main menu, click Options > Environment . In the open dialog, select General > Display .

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422309 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00418: (2026x) Structure

- page_id: `254422309`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422309/2026x+Structure
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:17:53.528+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This section provides procedures for structure modeling to represent how parts are decomposed, interconnected, and classified.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="p1">This section provides procedures for structure modeling to represent how parts are decomposed, interconnected, and classified.</p><p class="p1"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422797 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00419: (2026x) Structured control actions

- page_id: `254422797`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422797/2026x+Structured+control+actions
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:58:07.036+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Actions > (2026x) Controlling actions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Structured control action usages are used to control the performance of nested action usages in a structured way. There are three kinds of structured control action usages:

- An if action usage evaluates a condition expression and then performs a then clause action usage if the expression evaluates to true , or, optionally, an else clause action usage if the expression evaluates to false .
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) If action' space=''] page.
- A while loop action usage performs a body clause action usage iteratively, as long as its while expression continues to evaluate to true and its until expression continues to evaluate to false .
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) While and for loop actions' space=''] page.
- A for loop action usage performs a body clause action usage iteratively, assigning a loop variable successively for each iteration to the values resulting from the evaluation of a sequence expression.
  - See the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) While and for loop actions' space=''] page.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Structured control action usages are used to control the performance of nested action usages in a structured way. There are three kinds of structured control action usages:</p><ul><li>An <strong>if</strong> <strong>action usage</strong> evaluates a condition expression and then performs a then clause action usage if the expression evaluates to <em>true</em>, or, optionally, an else clause action usage if the expression evaluates to <em>false</em>.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) If action" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[If action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li>A <strong>while loop action usage </strong>performs a<strong> </strong>body clause action usage iteratively, as long as its while expression continues to evaluate to <em>true</em> and its until expression continues to evaluate to <em>false</em>.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) While and for loop actions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[While and for loop actions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li><li>A <strong>for loop action usage </strong>performs a<strong> </strong>body clause action usage iteratively, assigning a loop variable successively for each iteration to the values resulting from the evaluation of a sequence expression.<ul><li>See the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) While and for loop actions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[While and for loop actions]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li></ul></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422950 space=SYSML2P version=2 -->
## PAGE 00420: (2026x) Style sheet conditions and styles tables

- page_id: `254422950`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422950/2026x+Style+sheet+conditions+and+styles+tables
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-10-14T15:27:03.739+02:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Views > (2026x) Symbol styles > (2026x) Custom style sheets
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

On this page, you can find the conditions and styles tables to use as a reference guide for the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Customizing style sheet components' space=''] and/or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating style sheet components' space=''] of rules for your style sheets.

- . Contains [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Custom style sheets' space=''] definitions, their ref items and value types.
- . Contains [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Custom style sheets' space=''] definitions, their attributes and value types, including primary style definitions and predefined style definitions.
- . Contains [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Custom style sheets' space=''] definitions, their attributes and value types, including primary compartment style definitions and predefined compartment style definitions

##### Style sheet rule conditions table

| Condition definitions { Condition ref item : value type }DS_Styles::CoreStylesComponents::Predicates | Applicable to |
| --- | --- |
| ByElementMetaclassConditionpart def ByElementMetaclassCondition :> Condition { ref item elementMetaclass [1..*] : Metaobjects::Metaobject;} | All element metaclassesViewUsage ViewDefinition PartDefinition PartUsage PortDefinition PortUsage UseCaseUsageUseCaseDefinition LiteralExpression CalculationUsageCalculationDefinition ExhibitStateUsage StateUsageStateDefinition PerformActionUsageActionUsageActionDefinitionEnumerationDefinitionEnumerationUsage AttributeDefinition AttributeUsage ReferenceUsage ConcernUsageConcernDefinition RequirementUsageRequirementDefinition ItemDefinition ItemUsage ConstraintUsageConstraintDefinitionOperatorExpression EventOccurrenceUsageOccurrenceUsageOccurrenceDefinition Package Documentation Comment Classifier NamespaceExampleAttributeUsage set as condition examplepart :>> condition : ByElementMetaclassCondition { ref item :>> elementMetaclass = (SysML::Systems::AttributeUsage.metadata);} |
| ByMetadataAnnotationConditionpart def ByMetadataAnnotationCondition :> Condition { ref item annotatingMetadataType [1..*] : Metaobjects::Metaobject;} | Any element metadataExampleMatadata Approval set as condition examplepart :>> condition : ByMetadataAnnotationCondition { ref item :>> annotatingMetadataType = 'Vehicle Metadata Definitions'::Approval.metadata;} |
| ByDiagramTypeConditionpart def ByDiagramTypeCondition :> Condition { ref item diagramType [1..*] : Metaobjects::Metaobject;} | All and view definitionsExampleGeneral View set as condition examplepart :>> condition : ByDiagramTypeCondition { ref item :>> diagramType = DS_Views::SymbolicViews::gv.metadata;} |
| FreeFormConditionpart def FreeFormCondition :> Condition { calc test { in element : Metaobjects::Metaobject; // the element a symbol represents in a diagram in diagram : Metaobjects::Metaobject; // the diagram where the symbol is placed at return : Boolean; // true if evaluated succesfully, false otherwise }} | Specific individual elements (a specific view usage (e.g., 'VCCU Requirements Table') or a specific element (e.g., part usage 'VCCU')Examplepart :>> condition : FreeFormCondition { calc :>> test { 'VCCU View'.metadata == diagram // OR element == VCCU.metadata }} |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422262 space=SYSML2P version=7 -->
## PAGE 00421: (2026x) Subclassification

- page_id: `254422262`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422262/2026x+Subclassification
- version_number: 7
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:50:55.626+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Relations > (2026x) Specializations
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Use the subclassification relationshipto specialize a definition element. The specialized definition inherits the features of the more general definition element and can add other features.

##### Subclassification

###### Subclassifying a definition via the Textual Editor

To subclassify a definition via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, identify the definition element that you want to be the specializing one.
2. Follow the element name with either one of the following:
  1. keyword specializes
  2. textual symbol :>
3. Follow the keyword with the name of the specialized definition element.
4. Click the **Synchronize**button. partdefVehicle;partdefDieselVehicle:>Vehicle;/* or */partdefDieselVehiclespecializesVehicle;

###### Subclassifying a definition via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To subclassify a definition via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on an element symbol executing the Set Superclassifier command.

###### Subclassifying a definition via the view palette

To subclassify a definition via the view palette

1. In the view palette, under the Specializations group, select subclassification .
2. Draw a line with the arrowhead going from the specific definition element to the general one. [ATTACHMENT filename='Subclassification.png']

###### Subclassifying a definitionvia the Create Relationship command

To subclassify a definition via the Create Relationship command

1. Right-click the element that you want to be the specializing one.
2. In the shortcut menu, click Create Relationship , then in the submenu, click Outgoing , and then in the submenu, click subclassification . The Element Picker dialog opens.
3. In the Element Picker dialog, do one of the following:
  1. In a view, click the element symbol you want to specialize. Hold Shift to select multiple elements.
  2. Click the Add [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to specialize and click OK .
  3. Drag and drop the needed element you want to specialize from the Containment tree onto the Element Picker .
4. In the Element Picker dialog, click OK .

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<strong>subclassification</strong>. The <strong>Element Picker </strong>dialog opens.</li><li>In the <strong>Element Picker </strong>dialog, do one of the following:<ol><li>In a view, click the element symbol you want to specialize. Hold Shift to select multiple elements. </li><li>Click the <strong>Add <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_button.png" /></ac:image> </strong>button. The element selection dialog opens. In the element selection dialog, select the element you want to specialize and click <strong>OK</strong>.</li><li>Drag and drop the needed element you want to specialize from the Containment tree onto the <strong>Element Picker</strong>.</li></ol></li><li>In the <strong>Element Picker</strong><strong> </strong>dialog, click <strong>OK</strong>. </li></ol>

- version_date: `2025-12-03T10:39:11.922+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Analysis & Verification > (2026x) Case
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you create the needed case for your model, you can specify it further. Cases are usually specified with:

- The subject of a case is modeled as a distinguished parameter, similarly to the subject of a requirement.
- The objective** of a case is modeled as a requirement usage to be satisfied by the performance of the case.
- A case definition or usage may also have one or more actor parameters that represent roles played by an entity external to the subject of the case but necessary to the specification of the case.

A case can have only one subject and objective.

##### Specifying subject, actors, and objective for case

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Textual Editor

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the case's body, do the following:
  1. To specify a subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  2. To specify an actor, declare the actor keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  3. To specify an objective, declare the objective keyword, then in the element body, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Annotations' space=''] .
2. Click the Synchronize button. casedefFaultRecovery{// case definition 
subjectsystem:AutomationSystem; // case subject 
actorengineer:Person; // case actor 
objective{ // case objective 
doc 
/* The engineer determines the cause of the systemfault and resolves it returning the system tonominal operation.*/ 
} 
}

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler on a case element symbol executing the **Create Subject,****Create Actor** ,**Create Objective**, or**Create Require Constraint of Objective** command.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select a case symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create. The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.
  - Select a case symbol and on the right side of the actors compartment, click the Create actor button. The new element is created and displayed in the actors compartment.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Specification panel

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for cases via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **actors**compartment section's bar, click the **Create actor**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422397 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00423: (2026x) Subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases

- page_id: `254422397`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422397/2026x+Subjects+actors+and+objectives+for+use+cases
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:55:56.539+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Behavior > (2026x) Use cases
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A use case is typically specified as a sequence of interactions between the subject and the various actors, which are all modeled as part usages. The objective of the use case is to provide an observable result of value to one or more of its actors.

A use case can have only one subject and objective.

##### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via Textual Editor

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the use case's body, do the following:
  1. To specify a subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  2. To specify an actor, declare the actor keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  3. To specify an objective, declare the objective keyword, then in the element body , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Annotations' space=''] .
2. Click the Synchronize button. usecasedef'Provide Transportation'{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; // use case subject 
actordriver:Person; // use case actor 
actorenvironment:Environment;objective{ // use case objective 
doc/* Transport driver and passengers from starting location to ending location. */ 
}}

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on a use case element symbol, executing the **Create Subject,** **Create Actor,** **Create Objective**, or**Create Require Constraint of Objective** command.
- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Drag and Drop handler' space=''] on the specific subject or actor displayed on the use case element symbol, executing the **Set Subsetted Feature** **** command.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select a use case symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create. The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.
  - Select a use case symbol and on the right side of the actors compartment, click the Create actor button. The new element is created and displayed in the actors compartment.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the Specification panel

To specify subjects, actors, and objectives for use cases via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the **actors**compartment section's bar, click the **Create actor**[IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254423042 space=SYSML2P version=3 -->
## PAGE 00424: (2026x) Subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements

- page_id: `254423042`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254423042/2026x+Subjects+actors+and+stakeholders+for+requirements
- version_number: 3
- version_date: `2025-12-03T08:52:06.691+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Requirements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you create the needed requirements for your model, you can specify them further. Requirements are usually specified with subject, actor, and stakeholder parameters.

- A subject is a distinguished parameter that identifies the entity on which the requirement is being specified.
- An **actor**parameter is a part usage representing an entity that plays a special role relative to therequirement definition or usage. A requirement may have multiple actors.
- A **stakeholder**parameter is a part usage representing a role played by an entity (usually a person, organization, or other group) having concerns related to the containing requirement. A requirement may have multiple stakeholders.

##### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Textual Editor

To specify subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the requirement's body, do any of the following:
  1. To specify the subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional)
  2. To specify the actor, declare the actor keyword, specify the element name, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  3. To specify the stakeholder, declare the stakeholder keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional).
2. Click the Synchronize button. requirementvehicleSpecification{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
 actor environment : Environment; 
 stakeholder client : Clients;}

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler on a requirement element symbol executing the **Create Actor**,**Create Subject**, or**Create Stakeholder** command.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the symbol compartment in a view

- Do one of the following:
  - Select a requirement symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create.
  - Select a requirement symbol and on the right side of the needed compartment, click the Create <element> button. The element is created and displayed in the appropriate compartment.

###### Specifying subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Specification panel

To specify subjects, actors, and stakeholders for requirements via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''].
2. Do one of the following:
  1. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu.
  2. In the needed element compartment's section bar, click the **Create <element>** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Requirements are usually specified with subject, actor, and stakeholder parameters.</span></span></p><ul><li>A <strong>subject </strong>is a distinguished parameter that identifies the entity on which <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=254422663 space=SYSML2P version=4 -->
## PAGE 00425: (2026x) Subjects, objectives, and return parameters for analysis cases

- page_id: `254422663`
- space_key: `SYSML2P`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/SYSML2P/pages/254422663/2026x+Subjects+objectives+and+return+parameters+for+analysis+cases
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-12-03T10:39:27.949+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) SysML v2 Plugin Documentation > (2026x) Model composition > (2026x) Analysis & Verification > (2026x) Analysis case
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you create the needed analysis case for your model, you can specify it further.An analysis case is comprised of a set of steps with an objective to evaluate a result about a subject of the analysis. Each step can be an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating actions' space=''], a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Calculation' space=''], or an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Analysis case' space=''].

Analysis cases are typically specified with:

- The subject of an analysis case is modeled as a distinguished parameter, similar to the subject of a requirement, identifying what is being analyzed.
- The objective is a requirement on the result of the analysis case, allowing both [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Assume constraint' space=''] and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Require constraint' space=''] constraints.
- The return parameter for the analysis result, as for a calculation.

A case can have only one subject, objective, and return parameter.

##### Specifying subject, objective, and return parametersfor analysis case

###### Specifying subjects, objectives, and return parameters for analysis cases via the Textual Editor

To specify subjects, objectives, and return parameters for analysis cases via the Textual Editor

1. In the Textual Editor, within the curly braces of the case's body, do the following:
  1. To specify a subject, declare the subject keyword, specify the element name , [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specializations' space=''] (optional).
  2. To specify an objective, declare the objective keyword, then in the element body, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Annotations' space=''] .
  3. To specify an analysis result, declare the return keyword followed by the result parameter.
2. Click the Synchronize button. analysisdefFuelEconomyAnalysis{ 
subjectvehicle:Vehicle; 
returnfuelEconomyResult:DistancePerVolumeValue; 
objectivefuelEconomyAnalysisObjective{ 
doc 
/*The objective of this analysis is to determine whether thesubject vehicle can satisfy the fuel economy requirement.*/ 
requirement:FuelEconomyRequirement; 
} 
// ... 
}

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

To specify subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the Drag and Drop handler in a view

- Drag and drop an element from the Containment tree or use its symbol's Drag and Drop handler on an analysis case element symbol executing the **Create Subject, Create Objective**, or **Create Require Constraint of Objective** command.

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the symbol compartment in a view

For more information, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Compartments' space=''] page.

To specify subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the symbol compartment in a view

- Select an analysis case symbol and click the Create Element button on it. In the dropdown menu, select the element you want to create. The new element is created and displayed in its appropriate compartment.

###### Specifying subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the Specification panel

To specify subjects and objectives for analysis cases via the Specification panel

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification panel' space=''].
2. In the Properties section bar, click the **Create Element** [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The shortcut menu appears. Click the needed element kind in the shortcut menu. The element is created in the appropriate compartment.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE
